statement.markdown: Given a binary string as input, we need to count the number of bits that are set (i.e., 1), invert them, and then count the number of bits that are set again. However, the provided code has a logical bug that leads to incorrect results. Fix the bug to make the function work correctly.
statement.inputFormat: A binary string composed only of 0s and 1s, with a minimum length of 1 and a maximum length of 10000.
statement.outputFormat: The expected count of bits after inverting and counting again.
statement.notes: The input string will always be a valid binary string.

constraints.description: Length of the input binary string

examples.explanation: The input binary string is 10111. The number of bits that are set is 4. Inverting them results in 00001, and counting the number of bits set in the inverted string gives us 1.

The input binary string is 11111. The number of bits that are set is 5. Inverting them results in 00000, and counting the number of bits set in the inverted string gives us 0. However, after re-inverting the bits, all of them become set, thus the count is 5.

hints.text: Review the logic of the provided code to identify the bug.

editorial.title: Fixing the Logical Bug
editorial.intuition: The issue lies in the way we count the bits after inverting them. We need to correctly invert the bits and then count the number of set bits.
editorial.optimalSolution: Create a new binary string by inverting the input string, then count the number of set bits in the new string.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correct solution should correctly count the number of bits set after inverting.
editorial.dryRun: Example run to illustrate the solution.
